title: Quarterback sacks

infobox.value: occurs when the [quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") (or another offensive player acting as a passer) is [tackled](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move) "Tackle (football move)") behind the [line of scrimmage](/wiki/Line_of_scrimmage "Line of scrimmage") before throwing a [forward pass](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass"), when the quarterback is tackled behind the line of scrimmage in the "[pocket](/wiki/Passing_pocket "Passing pocket")" and without clear intent, or when a passer runs out of bounds behind the line of scrimmage due to defensive pressure.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This often occurs if the opposing team's [defensive line](/wiki/Defensive_team "Defensive team"), [linebackers](/wiki/Linebacker "Linebacker") or [defensive backs](/wiki/Defensive_backs "Defensive backs") are able to apply **pass pressure** (also called a **[pass rush](/wiki/Pass_rush "Pass rush")**) to quickly get past [blocking](/wiki/Blocking_(American_football) "Blocking (American football)") players of the [offensive team](/wiki/Offensive_team "Offensive team") (the quarterback's protection), or if the quarterback is unable to find an available [eligible receiver](/wiki/Eligible_receiver "Eligible receiver") (including [wide receivers](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver"), [running backs](/wiki/Running_back "Running back") and [tight ends](/wiki/Tight_end "Tight end")) to catch the ball, allowing the defense a longer opportunity to tackle the quarterback.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Quarterback sacks](/w/index.php?title=Quarterback_sacks&redirect=no "Quarterback sacks"))

Action in gridiron football

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Washington_Football_Team_vs._Detroit_Lions_at_Ford_Field%2C_Detroit%2C_Michigan%2C_November_15%2C_2020.jpg/330px-Washington_Football_Team_vs._Detroit_Lions_at_Ford_Field%2C_Detroit%2C_Michigan%2C_November_15%2C_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washington_Football_Team_vs._Detroit_Lions_at_Ford_Field,_Detroit,_Michigan,_November_15,_2020.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Jon_Bostic_Jonathan_Allen_Matt_Stafford_Kevin_Pierre-Louis_%2850611231312%29.jpg/330px-Jon_Bostic_Jonathan_Allen_Matt_Stafford_Kevin_Pierre-Louis_%2850611231312%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jon_Bostic_Jonathan_Allen_Matt_Stafford_Kevin_Pierre-Louis_(50611231312).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Jon_Bostic_Jonathan_Allen_Matt_Stafford_Kevin_Pierre-Louis_%2850610372493%29.jpg/330px-Jon_Bostic_Jonathan_Allen_Matt_Stafford_Kevin_Pierre-Louis_%2850610372493%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jon_Bostic_Jonathan_Allen_Matt_Stafford_Kevin_Pierre-Louis_(50610372493).jpg)

Quarterback [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford") being sacked by multiple [Washington Football Team](/wiki/Washington_Football_Team "Washington Football Team") players.

In [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football"), a **sack** occurs when the [quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") (or another offensive player acting as a passer) is [tackled](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move) "Tackle (football move)") behind the [line of scrimmage](/wiki/Line_of_scrimmage "Line of scrimmage") before throwing a [forward pass](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass"), when the quarterback is tackled behind the line of scrimmage in the "[pocket](/wiki/Passing_pocket "Passing pocket")" and without clear intent, or when a passer runs out of bounds behind the line of scrimmage due to defensive pressure.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This often occurs if the opposing team's [defensive line](/wiki/Defensive_team "Defensive team"), [linebackers](/wiki/Linebacker "Linebacker") or [defensive backs](/wiki/Defensive_backs "Defensive backs") are able to apply **pass pressure** (also called a **[pass rush](/wiki/Pass_rush "Pass rush")**) to quickly get past [blocking](/wiki/Blocking_(American_football) "Blocking (American football)") players of the [offensive team](/wiki/Offensive_team "Offensive team") (the quarterback's protection), or if the quarterback is unable to find an available [eligible receiver](/wiki/Eligible_receiver "Eligible receiver") (including [wide receivers](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver"), [running backs](/wiki/Running_back "Running back") and [tight ends](/wiki/Tight_end "Tight end")) to catch the ball, allowing the defense a longer opportunity to tackle the quarterback.

A sack is advantageous for the defending team as the offense loses a [down](/wiki/Down_(gridiron_football) "Down (gridiron football)"), and the line of scrimmage retreats several yards.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Even better for the defense is a sack causing the quarterback to [fumble](/wiki/Fumble "Fumble") the ball at or behind the line of scrimmage; this is also known as a strip sack and can result in a [turnover](/wiki/Turnover_(gridiron_football) "Turnover (gridiron football)") if the defense manages to obtain the ball. A quarterback who is pressured but avoids a sack can still be adversely affected by being forced to hurry.

The quarterback must pass the statistical line of scrimmage to avoid the sack. If a passer is sacked in his own end zone, the result is a [safety](/wiki/Safety_(gridiron_football_score) "Safety (gridiron football score)") and the defending team is awarded two points. If the football is fumbled and recovered either inside the end zone by the defense, or outside the end zone and is returned to the end zone this results in a [touchdown](/wiki/Touchdown "Touchdown") for the defense.

## Statistical record rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quarterback_sack&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Statistical record rules")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Patrick_Kerney_sacks_Aaron_Rodgers.jpg/250px-Patrick_Kerney_sacks_Aaron_Rodgers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patrick_Kerney_sacks_Aaron_Rodgers.jpg)

[Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") quarterback [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers") being sacked by [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") defensive end [Patrick Kerney](/wiki/Patrick_Kerney "Patrick Kerney") in 2009.

To be considered a sack, the quarterback must intend to throw a forward pass. If the play is designed for the quarterback to rush (run) the ball, any loss is subtracted from the quarterback's rushing total (and the play is ruled a [tackle for loss](/wiki/Tackle_for_loss "Tackle for loss") as opposed to a sack). If the quarterback's intent is not obvious, statisticians use certain criteria, such as the offensive line blocking scheme, to decide. Unique situations where a loss reduces a quarterback's rushing total (not a sack) are "[kneel downs](/wiki/Quarterback_kneel "Quarterback kneel")" (used to run time off the game clock).

A player will receive credit for half of a sack when multiple players contribute to the sacking of a quarterback, even if more than two players contributed.

In the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), it is possible to record a sack for zero yards. The [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") subtracts yards lost due to sacks from teams' passing totals (though the quarterback's individual passing total stats remain unchanged),[[3]](#cite_note-3) while the [NCAA](/wiki/College_football "College football") subtracts sack yardage from individual rushing totals.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quarterback_sack&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Cougars_on_offense_at_BYU_at_Air_Force_2010-09-11.jpg/250px-Cougars_on_offense_at_BYU_at_Air_Force_2010-09-11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cougars_on_offense_at_BYU_at_Air_Force_2010-09-11.jpg)

[BYU](/wiki/BYU_Cougars_football "BYU Cougars football") quarterback Riley Nelson being sacked by [Air Force](/wiki/Air_Force_Falcons_football "Air Force Falcons football").

The term *sack* was first popularized in the 1960s by [Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") defensive end [Deacon Jones](/wiki/Deacon_Jones "Deacon Jones"), who felt that a quarterback being sacked devastated the offense in the same way that [a city was devastated when it was sacked](/wiki/Looting#In_armed_conflict "Looting").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1999, Jones provided a *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* reporter with some other detailed imagery about his forte: "You take all the offensive linemen and put them in a [burlap](/wiki/Burlap "Burlap") bag, and then you take a baseball bat and beat on the bag. You're sacking them, you're bagging them. And that’s what you're doing with a quarterback."[[7]](#cite_note-TimesObit-7)

According to former NFL coach [Marv Levy](/wiki/Marv_Levy "Marv Levy"), [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") coach [George Allen](/wiki/George_Allen_(American_football_coach) "George Allen (American football coach)") may have coined the term when referring to [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") quarterback [Craig Morton](/wiki/Craig_Morton "Craig Morton")[[8]](#cite_note-8) when he purportedly stated before a game, "Before we play those Dallas Cowboys, we’re going to take that Morton salt and pour him into a sack."

Prior to *sack*, the term *dump* was often used, as the NFL's statistical office recorded all sacks under "dumping the passer".[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

The NFL only began to keep track of times passers lost yardage in 1961[[9]](#cite_note-9) and no credit was given to the defensive player responsible until 1982.[[10]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-10) Researcher John Turney of the [Pro Football Researchers Association](/wiki/Pro_Football_Researchers_Association "Pro Football Researchers Association") estimated that Jones recorded 173½ sacks in his career.[[11]](#cite_note-branch-11)

Controversial NFL rule changes made for the [2018 season](/wiki/2018_NFL_season "2018 NFL season") prohibit tacklers from landing on the quarterback after making a sack, with the punishment being a [roughing the passer](/wiki/Roughing_the_passer "Roughing the passer") penalty.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Pass pressure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quarterback_sack&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pass pressure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Nate_Longshore_under_pressure_AFB_071231-F-7061J-010.JPEG/250px-Nate_Longshore_under_pressure_AFB_071231-F-7061J-010.JPEG)](/wiki/File:Nate_Longshore_under_pressure_AFB_071231-F-7061J-010.JPEG)

A quarterback under pressure.

Of all forms of defensive pressure against the opposition's passer, sacks provide the most immediate impact by ending the offensive play.[[13]](#cite_note-schatz-13) However, quarterbacks sometimes avoid a sack by throwing an [incomplete pass](/wiki/Incomplete_pass "Incomplete pass") or risking an [interception](/wiki/Interception "Interception").[[14]](#cite_note-14) According to [Football Outsiders](/wiki/Football_Outsiders "Football Outsiders"), a quarterback hurry is the most common form of pass pressure. In the [2009 NFL season](/wiki/2009_NFL_season "2009 NFL season"), there were 1,106 sacks and 3,268 hurries, and a hurried quarterback generally averaged fewer yards per pass play compared to the average pass play.[[13]](#cite_note-schatz-13)

## NFL records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quarterback_sack&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: NFL records")]

These records are from 1982 onwards, the year the NFL started officially recording sacks.[[10]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-10)

* [NFL single-season sacks](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_annual_sacks_leaders "List of National Football League annual sacks leaders"): 23, [Myles Garrett](/wiki/Myles_Garrett "Myles Garrett"), 2025
* [NFL career sacks](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_career_sacks_leaders "List of National Football League career sacks leaders"): 200, [Bruce Smith](/wiki/Bruce_Smith "Bruce Smith"), 1985–2003
* NFL single-game sacks: 7, [Derrick Thomas](/wiki/Derrick_Thomas "Derrick Thomas"), November 11, 1990 vs. Seattle Seahawks[[note 1]](#cite_note-16)
* NFL sacks, rookie season: 14.5, [Jevon Kearse](/wiki/Jevon_Kearse "Jevon Kearse"), 1999
* NFL seasons leading league in sacks: 3, [T. J. Watt](/wiki/T._J._Watt "T. J. Watt"), 2020, 2021, 2023[[16]](#cite_note-17)
* NFL seasons with 20.0 or more sacks: 2, [J. J. Watt](/wiki/J._J._Watt "J. J. Watt"), 2012 & 2014[[17]](#cite_note-18)
* NFL most consecutive games recording a sack: 11, [Chris Jones](/wiki/Chris_Jones_(defensive_tackle,_born_1994) "Chris Jones (defensive tackle, born 1994)"), 2018
* NFL most consecutive games recording a sack (team): 75, [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers"), 2016–2021[[18]](#cite_note-19)
* [NFL career sacks taken](/wiki/List_of_most_sacked_NFL_quarterbacks "List of most sacked NFL quarterbacks"): 571, [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers"), 2005–present[[19]](#cite_note-20)
* NFL single-season sacks taken: 76, [David Carr](/wiki/David_Carr_(American_football) "David Carr (American football)"), 2002[[20]](#cite_note-21)
* NFL game sacks taken: 12, [Warren Moon](/wiki/Warren_Moon "Warren Moon"), September 29, 1985 and [Donovan McNabb](/wiki/Donovan_McNabb "Donovan McNabb"), September 30, 2007[[21]](#cite_note-22)
* NFL [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") most sacks in a single game: 12, [Carolina vs. Denver, 50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50") (7 by Denver, 5 by Carolina)
* NFL Super Bowl most sacks by a player in a single game: 3[[note 2]](#cite_note-24)

:   * [Reggie White](/wiki/Reggie_White "Reggie White") – [Green Bay vs. New England, XXXI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXI "Super Bowl XXXI")
    * [Darnell Dockett](/wiki/Darnell_Dockett "Darnell Dockett") – [Arizona vs. Pittsburgh, XLIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIII "Super Bowl XLIII")
    * [Kony Ealy](/wiki/Kony_Ealy "Kony Ealy") – [Carolina vs. Denver, 50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50")
    * [Grady Jarrett](/wiki/Grady_Jarrett "Grady Jarrett") – [Atlanta vs. New England, LI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LI "Super Bowl LI")

* NFL Super Bowl most sacks, career *(sacks compiled since XVII)*

:   * 4.5, [Charles Haley](/wiki/Charles_Haley "Charles Haley") – 5 games [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") [XXIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXIII "Super Bowl XXIII"), [XXIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXIV "Super Bowl XXIV"), [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") [XXVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXVII "Super Bowl XXVII"), [XXVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXVIII "Super Bowl XXVIII"), [XXX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXX "Super Bowl XXX")
    * 4.5, [Von Miller](/wiki/Von_Miller "Von Miller") – 2 games [Denver](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos") [50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50") and [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams") [LVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI "Super Bowl LVI")[[23]](#cite_note-25)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quarterback_sack&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* *[The Blind Side: Evolution of a Game](/wiki/The_Blind_Side:_Evolution_of_a_Game "The Blind Side: Evolution of a Game")* – non-fiction book by [Michael Lewis](/wiki/Michael_Lewis "Michael Lewis")

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Quarterback_sack&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** [Norm Willey](/wiki/Norm_Willey "Norm Willey") made 17 sacks during one game according to 1952 newspaper accounts. However, sacks weren't an official statistic at the time.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** [L. C. Greenwood](/wiki/L._C._Greenwood "L. C. Greenwood") had 4 sacks in [Super Bowl X](/wiki/Super_Bowl_X "Super Bowl X").[[22]](#cite_note-23) However, sacks were not officially recorded by the NFL at that point.
